name: Silas Weir
whyHere: You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Preserved Trail Parcel
inventory.description: Aldric's smokehouse goods — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped and knotted. Holds a week on the road. Two parcels.

inventory.name: Assembled Mid-Weight Spear
inventory.description: A seasoned ash shaft six feet long with the mid-weight iron tip road-lashed at the socket throat — wide-cut blade, cross-guard shoulder, oiled leather binding. Four threads seated: ironsense as anchor, Huginn's Ashwarden ending-sense through the fuller, a shadow-harm focus at the quillon, and an order-sense capacitor closed on Pell's reading idiom. The maker's mark is Pell's. Silas's…
inventory.provenance: Rune-bound at the Fell Pell on World-day 18 evening, with Huginn as witness at the stone ring.

inventory.name: Boar Tusks (pair)
inventory.description: Ivory-yellow and dense at the base — a matched pair from the scrub oak ridge bull. Raw material for something, not yet decided.

inventory.name: Valve Failure Timeline (note)
inventory.description: A small torn page in Mara's hand — four sleeve blank sales, four failure dates, two customer names. World-day minus-eighteen: Fen's intake. World-day minus-nine: east furlong farmer.

inventory.name: Contact Names Scrap (Sorel, Leth)
inventory.description: A chalk-written scrap from Mara — two names from the old pass circuit with Edge District ties. Sorel: spice-broker, wintered in the Edge District two years. Leth: archivist type, likely still there.

inventory.name: Hard Tallow Block
inventory.description: Rendered and pressed flat — good for leather, boots, rope-ends, and a dozen trail needs.

inventory.name: Oilcloth Fold
inventory.description: Heavy enough to shed a night of rain, light enough to pack small.

inventory.name: Pine-Tar Salve Tin
inventory.description: Dara's recipe — for cracked skin, rope-burn, and anything the cold gets into.

inventory.name: River Whetstone (Pell's)
inventory.description: A smooth river-flat whetstone — Pell's own. It belongs to the bench beside the sword now.

inventory.name: Fendt's Testimony (folded page)
inventory.description: A single page in Fendt's hand — dates, seventh-band designations, the nine-day gap, Grael named as the receiving authority, and the preliminary concern notice receipt date. Now sealed inside the document roll alongside Silas's chain-record, bound with brass wire and Calvar's wax. One copy. The other
inventory.provenance: Written by Fendt; carried by Silas from the Edge District; now sealed with a second hand into a complete record.

inventory.name: Brass Wire Coil (fine)
inventory.description: A coil of fine brass wire from Fendt's shop — used for sealing sample vessels; precise enough for binding or rune-work.

inventory.name: Chalk Dust Tin (wax-sealed)
inventory.description: Brilliant white chalk dust in a sealed tin — for marking surfaces in poor light or wet conditions.

inventory.name: Pre-Transition Filter Cloth
inventory.description: A tight-woven square of filtering cloth, pre-Transition manufacture — can strain contaminated water through multiple passes.

inventory.name: Pilfered Camp Tin
inventory.description: A small tin from the opportunists' pack — likely taken from someone's unattended camp. Contents unknown.

inventory.name: Trade-Knives (pair)
inventory.description: Two small belt-trade knives confiscated from the road opportunists — plain, serviceable, nothing special.

deeds.description: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Struck a fair provisioning arrangement with Aldric the smokehouse man — hunting for the village in exchange for food and supplies, and offered to keep watch on what's damaging the river catch.
Silas Weir bound death-runes into the assembled spear at an open-sky fire, with Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn witness at the stone ring.
Coordinated a clean boar kill in the north wood — Corvin landed the strike, Silas held the line and read the ending with a bound spear
Brought down the bull boar on the scrub oak ridge with Pell — a clean hunt despite a misfire in the rune-work.
Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.
Named the repaired death-bound spear Memory in Pell's forge at dawn — a word that held.
Kissed Pell Marsh at the forge bench at dawn — after a full night repairing Memory together and naming it over old wine.
Persuaded Fendt to commit his testimony to paper and walk to Millbrook — moved the falsified ledger thread from evidence-held to evidence-carried.
Persuaded Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer and author of the seventh-band specification — to leave the Edge District and travel to Millbrook to attest against Overseer Grael
Neutralized a relay figure in the cut-lane without violence or alarm — The Ended Threat, Wildcraft, and The Shadow Work braided into a clean exit for Calvar and the document.
Showed mercy to road opportunists after disarming them — named the broad man's grief, gave them provisions, and sent them to honest work rather than leaving them in the mud
Called the key players of the water crisis to Pell's forge for an evening council — Dara, Aldric, Fendt, Mara, and the engineer Calvar, all in one room.
Organized the valley's water-crisis response into four parallel working threads — engineering, logistics, supply, and accounts — with named leads for each, in front of every stakeholder
Sparred Pell at the waygate at first light — belt knife against rune-read blade, came out of it with one real lesson and two feet she said were good
Won a Coliseum bout with a single spoken word — never pressed the weakness, let the opponent decide

chronicle: The Fell Pell gathering sealed and delegated — Calvar to repair lead, Dara to logistics, Mara to supply and comms, Aldric to accounts, Fendt named to the fellowship, document roll complete and bound for Pip Cotter. Silas and Pell step out into the cold night, the road beginning tomorrow, the forge warm behind them.

The Fell Pell gathering: Pell's brigandine finished with full protective rune-work collar to hem, one radiant mark at the chest from the vial's last scraping, and a partial Shielding Word seated in its outer ring with the promise of completion. Pell's short sword rune-bound with four threads including Huginn's ending-sense and her own ironsense. The forge named the Fell Pell; the fellowship formally opened. Calvar took the repair lead, Dara the logistics, Mara supply and comms, Aldric the accounts; Fendt named to the fellowship and vouched for by Aldric. The document roll sealed with every witness line present, Pip Cotter named as carrier. Silas and Pell settled the road: south lane in the morning, home first, then the Disputed Zone. The coals cooled. The night was quiet.
Silas and Pell sleep, share a last morning meal at the forge, establish a relay protocol with Mara Wells, say their farewells, and step south out of Millbrook at a measured morning hour.
Silas, Pell, and Huginn step through the waygate together at dawn — the road south beginning in earnest.

turns.player: Take a moment in the plaza — use Order-Sense to read the Hub's design and what it is part of before walking into an unfamiliar city
turns.summary: Silas reads the Hub's design at the waygate — a deliberate geometry, seven calibration gates, a measuring function at its center. The lattice is too thin for the full picture.
turns.narration:
You set your hand against the left upright of the waygate arch and let Order-Sense open. The lattice here is thin — you can feel it immediately, the craft running lean, like a lamp with too little oil. What comes back is partial and costs you.

The Hub is not a city that grew. It was placed — a deliberate geometry, seven gates arranged at angles that serve something older than the roads connecting them. The arch your hand is on is one of seven, and all seven are load-bearing in a way that has nothing to do with stone. They are reference points. Calibration marks. Whatever the Hub was designed to do at its center, these gates are how it oriented itself to do it.

The design's full purpose stays just out of reach. The lattice is too thin here, and you are running on its edge. You get the shape of something — a measuring function, a hub in the literal sense, something that once took readings from multiple directions at once — but not the what or the why. It is like reading the frame of a tool without the tool itself.

Pell has already stepped through and is standing at the plaza's edge, pack settled, watching you with the patient attention of someone who knows when a read is finishing. Huginn lands on the waygate's crossbar above your hand, looks south into the city, and says nothing. Which is, in its way, a comment.

turns.player: As far as I understand it... these Waygates connect the various corners of the world - some cult maintains them... but we'll need to find which one will take us to the lands of the Ashwardens.  No rush though - we have a whole city in front of us - a coliseum to test our mettle in, questions we could run for the Council of Maven's - or even ask them to assist the Valley.  They basically have everything here - point out the various entities you can see from here, including the Ent and the Elves.
turns.summary: Silas surveys the Hub from the waygate plaza at dawn — the Council of Mavens, the Coliseum, an ancient Ent, and a small Elven camp all visible from the crescent stones.
turns.narration:
From the plaza crescent, with the waygate stones at your back and the dawn light coming in flat and pale from the east, the Hub opens up to the north in tiers. It is not one thing. It is many things arranged so they do not have to speak to each other if they do not want to.

The nearest landmark is a broad stone colonnade, its pillars carved in a style older than any road leading to it — you will learn it is the Hall of the Council of Mavens, the body that arbitrates disputes between the peoples who pass through or settle here. Their seal is a set of balanced scales over an open eye, carved into the lintel facing the plaza. A clerk's board outside the main door shows petitions currently in queue. People are already moving in and out, early as it is, which tells you the Council's hours are not polite ones. Further along the northern tier, half-hidden behind a broad market row of covered stalls, the Coliseum rises — not a ruin and not a relic, but a working structure, its outer wall showing the fresh soot-marks of recent use. You can hear it faintly: a rhythmic impact, stone on stone or iron on shield, someone already at practice before the city has properly woken. The gates are open.

To the east, past a watercarrier's junction, stands a structure that makes Pell stop looking where she was looking. Said Pell quietly, 'That is an Ent.' And it is — a living tree, ancient and deliberate, rooted in a wide circle of cleared ground ringed by low stone. It is not a tree that wound up here. It is standing here on purpose, and the bark of its lower trunk shows the pale scars of old carvings, healed over, that mark it as someone who has been in conversation with the world for a long time. Two smaller figures rest in its lower branches. At this distance you cannot tell if they are sleeping or listening. On the plaza's west side, closer to you, a group of four people in long pale traveling coats have settled on the paving and laid out a camp with a quiet efficiency that suggests they have done it a hundred times. Their ears are long. Their gear is immaculate. They have not looked at you yet, but you have the clear sense they knew you were there before you stepped through. The Elves of the Hub are not numerous, but they are present, and they are paying attention. Huginn shifts on the crossbar above your head and cracks one eye toward them, then says nothing. The silence after is pointed.

turns.player: Cross to the Elven camp — introduce yourself and ask about the waygate network, specifically whether one connects to Ashwarden lands
turns.summary: Siol of the Elves names the sixth waygate as the path to the Ashwarden March and tells Silas that Marrow's presence marks him before any introduction.
turns.narration:
You cross the plaza at a measured walk, Memory road-lashed at your back and Huginn riding your left shoulder like a gray-black pennant. The Elven camp is small — four travelers in long pale coats, their gear stacked in clean columns on the west paving, a low oil lamp still burning against the dawn cold. They are not surprised to see you coming. One of them, the tallest, rises before you are halfway across. She does not rush, but she is standing by the time you arrive.

She is a woman of perhaps forty human years — though that means little with the Elves — with the kind of stillness that is not indifference but attention held very quiet. Her coat is the color of winter birch, her hair pulled back and braided with a single dark cord. She looks at Huginn first, then at you, and her expression settles into something that is close to recognition, though you have never met.

"Ashwarden," she says, in common speech with an accent that rounds the consonants softly. "Or close enough to it that Marrow rides with you." She uses the companion name without being told it. "I am Siol. We came through the eastern gate two days ago, from the Steadwood passage." She gestures toward one of the seven arches. "The gate you want is the sixth, counting from the south lane — the one with the lower lintel and the darker stone. The Ashwarden March lies at its far end." She pauses, reading you with those quiet eyes. "Though the March does not always receive travelers the way a city does. Someone there will know you are coming before you arrive."

She offers this without editorializing — neither a warning nor a welcome. Plain information, plainly given. Pell has moved up quietly beside you. Huginn says nothing, but shifts its weight once on your shoulder, which is agreement enough.

turns.player: Introduce yourself honorably, as well as your Soul Wife, Pell Marsh.  Give Huginn a chance to introduce himself if he would like.  Then enquire about the Steadwood passage and if you can be of any assistance.
turns.summary: Silas introduces himself, Pell, and Huginn to Siol at the Elven camp — she names the Steadwood passage as clear and warns that the March will know of Huginn before Silas arrives.
turns.narration:
You introduce yourself plainly, one hand open at your side. 'Silas Weir,' you say, 'of Millbrook, and before that of the road.' You turn slightly so Pell is beside you rather than behind you. 'And Pell Marsh — my soul wife, and the smith who made the mark on that blade at my back.' Siol's attention moves to Pell with genuine interest, neither hurried nor studied. She gives Pell a nod that carries weight — the kind given to someone recognized, not merely introduced.

You lift your left hand slightly, an invitation toward Huginn. The bird turns one yellow eye on Siol, then opens his beak without urgency. What comes out is not quite a croak and not quite a word, but it is unmistakably deliberate. Then he settles his feathers and looks away, as though the matter is concluded. Siol's expression does not change, but something in her shoulders eases — the faint shift of a person who has received the answer they were waiting for.

'Marrow,' she says, addressing the bird directly, 'it has been some years.' Huginn does not respond. Siol does not press him.

She returns to you then and says, 'I came through the Steadwood passage two days ago. The eastern gate here.' She glances back at the sixth arch — lower lintel, darker stone. 'The passage is clear. The road from this end to the Steadwood mouth is three days if the weather holds, fewer if it does not slow you.' She pauses, as though weighing something. Then she adds, with the same level tone, 'The March does not hold a welcome gate the way the Hub does. When you arrive, they will already know what walks with you.' She means Huginn. She means this as useful information, not a warning.

turns.player: Tell her you would love to hear about her acquaintance with Marrow some time...   Look to Pell to see if she has anything to ask Siol.
turns.summary: Silas invites future conversation about Marrow; Pell asks Siol about the sixth gate — whether there is a toll or keeper on the other side.
turns.narration:
You tell Siol that you would love to hear about her acquaintance with Marrow some time — when the road allows it. You say it without pressure, leaving the door open rather than pushing through it. Siol receives the offer with a small tilt of her head, the way a person does when they file something for later rather than closing it. 'The road tends to allow what it intends,' she says quietly.

You turn to Pell then, catching her eye — an invitation, not a prompt. She has been reading Siol the whole time with the same steady attention she gives an unfamiliar alloy: patient, thorough, not yet decided. She holds the look for a moment, then turns back to Siol. 'The sixth gate,' Pell says. 'The one with the lower lintel. Is there a toll, or a keeper, or anything waiting on the other side we should know before we walk through?' Her voice is practical. She is not asking to be polite. She wants to know.

Huginn turns his head slowly from Pell to Siol and back, as if weighing which answer he will find more interesting.

turns.player: Listen to what Siol knows about the sixth gate's other side
turns.summary: Siol answers Pell's question: no toll, no keeper — but the March reads who comes through, and Huginn's presence will be felt before Silas steps out of the arch.
turns.narration:
Siol turns her attention fully to Pell, and the small shift in her posture says she expected the question. 'There is no toll,' she says. 'And no keeper at the threshold — not in the way you mean.' She pauses a moment, choosing her next words with the care of someone who has learned that precision matters more than speed. 'The March does not keep a welcome gate because it does not need one. The land itself reads who comes through, and Marrow —' she glances at Huginn, brief and direct — 'Marrow is known there the way a name is known. Before you step out of the arch on the far side, they will already have felt something arrive.'

Huginn says nothing. He turns his head away from Siol and looks south, toward the sixth gate, and the tilt of his neck is the comment.

Siol continues, still addressing Pell as much as Silas. 'The Steadwood passage road from here runs three days to the March mouth, if weather holds. The road is clear — I came through it two days past without incident.' She looks at the spear lashed at Silas's back, then back to his face. 'You will not be stopped. But you will be seen arriving, and whoever greets you will already know something of what you carry.' She says that without alarm, simply as a fact the road requires you to hold.

Pell takes that in without changing her expression. Then she nods once — the same nod she gives a structural answer that checks out. She looks at Silas and says nothing, but the question she carried is settled.

turns.player: Ask if Siol herself is bound somewhere, and whether her road and yours run the same direction for any part of it
turns.summary: Silas asks Siol if their roads overlap — she answers yes, for a day or so heading south, if the pace is easy.
turns.narration:
You ask Siol plainly whether she is herself bound somewhere, and whether any stretch of your roads runs the same direction.

She considers the question without hurry. 'South,' she says at last, 'but not as far as the March mouth. My road turns before the Steadwood thins.' She tilts her head slightly, the gesture of someone mapping two lines against each other in their thoughts. 'For a day or so, perhaps, if the pace is easy. I do not keep a strict morning schedule.'

Huginn shifts on your shoulder and makes a sound — not quite approval, not quite skepticism, but something that Siol notices. She glances at him. 'Marrow does not object,' she says, dry and quiet, and then she looks back at you to see whether you do.

Pell says nothing. She is watching you, the same way she watches a joint being brought together — to see whether the fit is clean.

turns.player: Invite her to travel with you as far as her roads goes - but also offer to allow her to give you a quick tour of what the city has to offer.  It's Pell's first time here and you are not well acquainted yourself.  You might like to try a duel and to petitiion the Council of Mavens.
turns.summary: Silas invites Siol to tour the Hub before the road south — she accepts without ceremony and leads the party toward the Coliseum first.
turns.narration:
You tell Siol yes — her company south as far as her road runs, and you mean it plainly. Then you add the second part: neither you nor Pell know this city well, and this is Pell's first time through a waygate. If she has no strict schedule and the morning is still young, you would welcome a short tour of what the Hub has to offer before the road takes you. You name the Coliseum and the Council of Mavens as things you have reason to visit.

Siol looks at Pell first, which is an interesting choice. Pell meets the look with a short nod — not warming up to her yet, but fair. Then Siol looks back at you. 'A tour is a reasonable use of a morning,' she says. She folds the edge of her coat back from her belt and turns to say something brief to the nearest of her camp-mates — a low exchange, a hand lifted in acknowledgment, and she steps clear of the camp without further ceremony.

Huginn makes a sound from your shoulder. It is the sound he makes when he has assessed a situation and found it, on balance, acceptable. Siol catches it. The corner of her mouth moves, just slightly. 'The Coliseum keeps its outer gates open before the market sets,' she says, starting toward the plaza's northern edge. 'The Council of Mavens takes petitions before midday. I would suggest the Coliseum first — the queue outside the petition board fills early, and the wait is more bearable if you have done something physical beforehand.' She says this as though it is simply a fact about the city's rhythms, and perhaps it is.

Pell falls into step beside you. The Hub is alive around you even at this hour — figures crossing the crescent stones with the purposeful ease of people who know exactly where they are going. The Ent's circle to the east is just visible between two market stalls that are unfolding their awnings. The Council's colonnade is ahead, its petition board a pale rectangle on the near pillar even from here.

turns.player: Ask Siol about the Ent's circle as you walk — what is it, and is it the kind of thing a traveler can approach
turns.summary: Siol names the Ent as Taur — ancient, present before the city, approachable but not reliably conversational. The party moves toward the Coliseum.
turns.narration:
You fall into step beside Siol as the party moves across the crescent stones, the Coliseum wall ahead and the market stalls opening to your right. The Ent's circle catches your eye to the east — a low ring of stone with something vast and patient rooted at its center, and two figures visible high in the branches. You ask Siol about it as you walk, keeping your voice easy.

She glances east without breaking her stride. 'The Ent has been here longer than the city,' she says. 'The circle was not built around it — the city grew toward it, carefully, and stopped at the right distance.' She lets that settle for a pace or two. 'His name is Taur. He chose this ground a long time ago and he has not said why, though people have asked.' A brief pause. 'He is approachable. He does not speak quickly, so visitors need patience, and he does not speak to everyone. But he is not unkind about it.'

Pell glances at the branches and the two figures there. 'People sleep in it?' she says.

'Some mornings,' Siol says. 'He allows it. Whether he invites it is a different question.' She angles her path slightly north toward the Coliseum outer wall, its soot-marks visible in the low dawn light. 'If you have time before the road, it is worth a few minutes to stand at the ring. Whether he speaks is his business, not yours.'

Huginn turns his head east toward the circle and holds it there for a moment — that particular stillness that is not indifference — and then faces forward again.

turns.player: Ask Siol what the Coliseum duels are like — what to expect before you step into one
turns.summary: Siol describes the Coliseum duels as honest bouts with declared crafts, a referee named Orvil, and yield as the end — the party reaches the outer wall.
turns.narration:
The Coliseum outer wall is close now — close enough to read the soot-marks on the stone above the nearest gate, and to hear, faintly, the sound of feet on packed sand inside. The gates stand open. A few figures move in and out at a measured pace, none of them in any hurry.

You ask Siol what the duels here are like. What to expect before you step into one.

She considers the question for a few steps before she answers. 'They are honest,' she says. 'That is the first thing. No hidden weapons, no interference from the gallery. The Coliseum keeps a referee — a compact man named Orvil, if he is still on the morning rotation — and Orvil does not look the other way.' She glances at Memory riding at your back. 'Spears are allowed. Most things are, as long as you declare them at the gate.' She pauses. 'They will ask you to name your craft before you enter the circle. Not as a condition — as a courtesy, so the person across from you knows the shape of what they are agreeing to.'

Pell says nothing, but her eyes move to the open gate with the attention she gives to things she is quietly deciding about.

Siol adds one more thing as you reach the wall's shadow. 'They do not fight to death here,' she says. 'Yield is the end. If someone is not going to yield, Orvil calls it.' She looks at Huginn. 'Your bird will be visible from the gallery. That may affect who chooses to step in across from you.'

turns.player: Let Pell say whether she wants to watch or walk the circle herself first — this is her first time here
turns.summary: Pell decides to walk the Coliseum floor before watching — the party prepares to step through the gate.
turns.narration:
You look to Pell and let the question sit easy between you — whether she wants to watch from the gallery first, or step down onto the sand herself.

She is quiet for a moment. Her eyes are on the open gate, reading it the way she reads a new piece of iron: not with excitement, but with the attention that comes before a decision. Then she says, 'I'll walk it.' She says it the way she says most things — without ceremony, as if she is simply reporting what she has already concluded. 'I want to see the floor before I see anyone else on it.'

Siol makes a small sound — not quite approval, but something adjacent. 'That is the sensible order,' she says, and she means it.

Huginn shifts on your shoulder and looks through the open gate. He is not commenting. He simply looks, with the calm patience of something that has attended more arrivals than it can count, and waits to see what this one becomes.

turns.player: Step through the gate with Pell — walk the floor together, read the space, and declare crafts at the gate if Orvil asks
turns.summary: The party steps through the Coliseum gate — Orvil logs their crafts, Pell walks the floor, and the sand of the morning's early bouts is still unsettled underfoot.
turns.narration:
You step through the gate together — Pell half a pace to your left, Huginn riding your shoulder, Siol just behind. The sound changes the moment you cross the threshold. Outside it was the open air and the market waking up. Inside it is packed sand underfoot and the low echo of a space that holds its noise close. The Coliseum is not as large as you expected, but it is well-used. The sand shows the marks of the morning's early bouts — feet, and occasionally knees, and one long scrape that ends abruptly in the middle of the circle. The gallery runs the full ring above, stone benches already holding a dozen or so watchers. The light comes in at a low angle through the open top, catching the dust still in the air.

A compact man is standing near the gate, not blocking it, but positioned to catch arrivals. He has a short baton tucked under his arm and the look of someone who has done this task so many times that the boredom left years ago and left behind something quieter and more alert. He takes in the party in one sweep — you, Pell, Huginn, Siol — and his gaze settles on Memory at your back. 'Crafts,' he says, without preamble. His voice is level, not unfriendly. 'And the spear stays peace-bound unless you're stepping in.'

Siol says, beside you, 'That is Orvil.' She does not say it for your benefit so much as to confirm the world is still in order.

You name your crafts at the gate, and Pell names hers. Orvil notes them with a small dip of the chin — neither impressed nor suspicious, just filed. He steps back a pace to let you pass. Pell moves out onto the sand first, and you follow. She does not rush it. She walks the near arc of the ring slowly, one hand loose at her side, the other thumb hooked in her belt, reading the floor the way she said she would — for what it is, before she sees anyone else on it. Huginn's eye follows the gallery above. From up there, the bird would be visible to every watcher in the ring. It knows this. It sits with the unhurried patience it brings to everything.

turns.player: Introduce yourself to Orvil and ask about entering a bout — who is here, what the shape of a match looks like this morning
turns.summary: Orvil gives Silas the shape of a morning bout and names a potential opponent — a threshold-walker in a grey coat, waiting at the far arc.
turns.narration:
Orvil sees you coming before you reach him. His eyes drop to Memory at your back, peace-bound as he required, then lift to your face — and then to Huginn, one last time, in the way of a man who has already filed the bird and moved on.

You give him your name. Silas Weir. Ashwarden craft, one spear, one companion that does not fight.

Orvil nods once. 'Weir,' he says, the way a man repeats a name to fix it. 'Morning bouts are short — three exchanges, yield or the sand says it for you. You declare your crafts to your opponent before the bout, same as you declared to me at the gate. No ambush, no surprise.' He glances at the ring, where Pell is still walking the near arc slowly, reading the floor. 'The gallery watches but does not interfere. I call it if a yield is missed.'

He tilts his chin toward a cluster of three figures near the far arc — two young men and a woman, all standing loose, all watching Pell with the patient attention of people who have been waiting for the floor to clear. 'Those three came in together,' Orvil says. 'The one in the grey coat has been asking after a bout since before the first light. He declared threshold-walking — something close to yours, by the sound of it. The other two are his seconds.' He looks back at you. 'You want me to put it to him, I will. Or you wait for your woman to finish her read and decide.'

turns.player: Ask Orvil to put it to the grey-coat — you're ready
turns.summary: Silas calls for the bout — Orvil crosses the sand, the grey-coat accepts, and the two opponents stand at the center-line for the craft declaration.
turns.narration:
You nod to Orvil and tell him plainly: put it to the grey-coat. You are ready.

Orvil does not waste words on it. He tucks his baton under his arm, crosses the sand in the easy walk of a man who has done this ten thousand times, and stops at the far arc. You cannot hear what he says from here, but the grey-coat turns, looks across the ring at you, and then says something short to one of the two seconds beside him. The seconds step back. The grey-coat hands over a water flask without looking at it — already done with that, eyes on the ring.

Pell has stopped walking. She is standing at the near arc, one thumb still at her belt, and she watches the grey-coat cross to the center-line without hurry. She reads him the way she reads everything — from the outside in, without rushing the conclusion.

Orvil comes back and plants himself between you, two paces left of the center-line. 'Bout between Silas Weir, Ashwarden craft, and' — he glances at the grey-coat — 'Tane Solr, threshold-walking.' He says it the way he says everything. Like a man reading from a page that is already written. Then he says, 'Declare your crafts to each other. Then we begin.'

turns.player: Declare honestly, then look at him with deathsense before the first exchange — read what is already declining in him
turns.summary: Silas declares crafts honestly to Tane Solr and opens deathsense — a quiet read that finds an old ankle injury in the threshold-walker: healed imperfectly, compensated automatically, a liability on a fast lateral step.
turns.narration:
You look at Orvil and say the crafts plainly. Ashwarden — deathsense, order-sense. The spear is Memory; it stays on your back unless the bout asks otherwise. Tane Solr listens without moving. He has the stillness of someone who has done this before and is not bothered by the catalogue.

Orvil nods and steps back. That is all the permission you need.

You let deathsense open — restrained, a quiet thing, the way you might hold a door open to hear what is on the other side without going through it. Tane comes clear immediately. He is a lean man in good health, road-fit, nothing catastrophically failing. But there it is, small and plain: his left ankle. Old damage, healed imperfectly — a ligament that was pulled and never fully reset. He compensates so automatically that he does not think about it anymore. On dry packed sand, it costs him almost nothing. On a lateral step, especially a fast one, it will cost him something.

You exhale through your nose and look at him the ordinary way again. He is watching you back with exactly the patience he has been showing since he first crossed to the center-line. He has declared threshold-walking, and you believe him — there is a quality of someone who has spent a great deal of time at edges, waiting for the right moment to step. The ankle will not end the bout for him. But it is there, and you know where it is.

turns.player: Shadow-step into his flank the moment Orvil says go — arrive where the ankle is already committed
turns.summary: Silas shadowsteps to Tane Solr's left flank on the opening call — arriving at the ankle without pressing it, the first exchange well-positioned.
turns.narration:
Orvil raises his baton and says go.

You do not move toward Tane Solr. You move through the shadow his own body casts on the sand — the one behind his left heel where he has already shifted his weight forward to meet you at center. The step is quiet and economic. You arrive at his left flank before the air has finished carrying the word.

His ankle is right there, already committed, already bearing more than it was built to take on a fast redirect. You do not press the weakness. You simply stand at the wrong angle for him to address quickly, Memory still on your back, giving him the shape of the problem without forcing it.

Tane Solr turns from the waist, not the feet. He is good enough to know better than to pivot on that ankle right now. He shows you a careful stance — weight redistributed, nothing hurried — and his eyes are steady. He is not rattled. He is assessing. From the near arc, Pell says nothing. Huginn says nothing.

turns.player: Speak — one quiet word to Tane while you have him at the wrong angle; let him decide whether to yield or continue on bad footing
turns.summary: Silas speaks one word — Ankle — and Tane Solr yields on his own terms. The bout ends without the weakness pressed.
turns.narration:
You let the moment sit for a breath — his weight committed, your position solid, the ankle already the question in the room. Then you say it, low enough that only Tane hears it. One word. 'Ankle.'

His eyes sharpen, and then something behind them settles into a different kind of attention. He has not moved yet. He is looking at you the way a careful person looks at something they underestimated — not angry, not embarrassed, just recalibrating.

For three full seconds he is still. Then he turns his head once, brief, toward Orvil — and gives a short downward pull of his chin.

Orvil's baton comes down. 'Yield,' he says, clear enough for the gallery. That is all. He does not say it twice.

A ripple goes through the dozen watchers above — not a cheer, something quieter than that. Recognition, maybe. The two seconds at the far arc exchange a look. One of them uncorks the water flask.

Pell lets out a breath at the near arc, slow and controlled. She does not say anything yet. She is still reading it.

turns.player: Walk over to Tane — acknowledge the bout properly, fighter to fighter
turns.summary: Silas crosses the sand to acknowledge Tane Solr properly — brief, honest, fighter to fighter. Tane names his curiosity rather than his embarrassment; Pell vouches without ceremony.
turns.narration:
You close the distance to Tane Solr at a walk — no hurry, no ceremony, Memory still on your back. The sand between you is three steps, maybe four.

You stop when you are close enough to speak without raising your voice. He is watching you with that new quality of attention — the kind that comes after something unexpected has happened and the mind is still filing it properly. His weight is back on both feet now, the ankle not favored but treated more carefully than it was a minute ago.

You say it plainly. Good bout. You gave me a clean read and you gave yourself a clean yield. That is not nothing.

Tane holds the look for a moment. Then the corner of his mouth shifts — not quite a smile, but the shape a smile would have if it did not want to commit to the name. He says, without any particular heat: I have been walking that ankle for three years and no one has ever named it in the first breath. He pauses. I should be embarrassed. I find I am mostly curious.

From the near arc, Pell speaks without looking up from whatever she has been reading in the exchange. She says to Tane: He does that. Holds his flask out at arm's length a moment later — offered in the straightforward way of someone who has just decided this person is worth a drink of water. You hear Huginn shift once on your shoulder, unhurried.

quests.title: The Edge District Ledger
quests.premise: A filtration supervisor named Fendt was marked on the Edge District accountability board by his own superior — and no accompanying record was filed. A keeper of thirty years says she has never seen that happen. Fendt is going to be ruined by a piece of paper that does not exist.
quests.stakes: If you walk away: Fendt is stripped and exiled within the season, the water-filtration failure he was blamed for goes uncorrected because the man who understood it is gone, and Overseer Grael's method — a name on a board, no record, no appeal — is confirmed as something that works. He will use it again. He has used it before.

stages.objective: Find out what the missing record would have said.
stages.condition: Speak with Fendt OR obtain the filtration log for the marked period.
stages.change: Codex: Fendt's account is recorded — and it does not match the board.

stages.objective: Find who marked him, and why there is no record.
stages.condition: Identify Overseer Grael as the marking authority (via the board's own chain, the keeper's testimony, or Grael himself).
stages.change: Codex: Grael is entered as an entity, linked to Fendt. He becomes aware that someone is looking.

stages.objective: Find out whether the marking is a pattern.
stages.condition: Locate at least one prior unrecorded marking (the keeper remembers; the archive half-remembers; Grael's own ledger will say if you can reach it).
stages.change: Codex: the pattern is named. Grael moves from 'an official' to 'a regional power'. He begins to act against you.

stages.objective: Decide what the truth is for.
stages.change: The district changes. So does Fendt. So, one way or another, does Grael.

routes.verist: Say it plainly, in public, at the board. Truth as a hammer.
routes.lattice: Take the ledger apart line by line until the arithmetic confesses. He is beaten by his own records.
routes.veilwright: Make a better record. Save Fendt with a truth you built. It works, and you know what you did.
routes.umbral: Harbor Fendt. Get him out. He lives; the district does not change.
routes.abyssal: Name what Grael actually wants — a quiet district and not to be the villain — and give him a way to have both, at a price.
routes.stillhold: Broker it. Fendt is restored quietly, Grael saves face, nobody is punished, and the method survives.

the-low-lamp-inn.name: The Low Lamp Inn

notes: [d4] Calvar arrived here with the rolled document after extraction from the cooperage — the party occupies the back corner booth. Maret Doss assigned it without being asked.

back-corner-booth.name: Back Corner Booth
back-corner-booth.note: Wall-backed, door in sight-line — the booth Maret Doss assigns to people she has learned not to ask about.

notes: [d6] The waygate opens onto the south lane — a shallow valley running south, tree line low, packed-dirt road with recent wheel ruts. First stop on the road from Millbrook toward the Disputed Zone.

[d6] Order-Sense partial read on dawn of World-day 18: the seven gates are calibration marks for a measuring function centered on the plaza. Design predates the roads. Full purpose unresolved — lattice too thin.

[d6] The Hub's major landmarks identified from the plaza at dawn: Council of Mavens colonnade (north), Coliseum (northwest behind market row), Ent circle (east), Elven camp (west paving). The city is active before full light.
[d6] The sixth waygate from the south lane — lower lintel, darker stone — is the connection to the Ashwarden March. Confirmed by Siol of the Elves.
[d6] The party entered and declared crafts at the gate — Orvil on morning rotation, sand showing early-bout marks, gallery occupied at dawn.

south-lane-beyond-the-waygate.name: South Lane beyond the Waygate
south-lane-beyond-the-waygate.note: Packed dirt road with wheel ruts, a shallow valley opening south, the waygate stones visible behind.

hub-waygate-plaza.name: Hub Waygate Plaza
hub-waygate-plaza.note: The open crescent of seven arches where travelers arrive — the city begins at its northern edge

council-of-mavens-hall.name: Council of Mavens Hall
council-of-mavens-hall.note: A broad stone colonnade on the Hub's near northern tier — petition board outside, scales-and-eye seal on the lintel. Open at dawn.

the-hub-coliseum.name: The Hub Coliseum
the-hub-coliseum.note: Working fighting ground northwest of the plaza, past the market row — fresh soot-marks on the outer wall, gates open before first light.

the-ent-s-circle.name: The Ent's Circle
the-ent-s-circle.note: A cleared ground ringed by low stone to the east of the plaza — an ancient Ent rooted here on purpose, healed bark-carvings on the lower trunk, two figures in the branches.

the-sixth-gate-ashwarden-passage.name: The Sixth Gate — Ashwarden Passage
the-sixth-gate-ashwarden-passage.note: Lower lintel and darker stone than the other arches — the gate that leads to the Ashwarden March, identified by Siol.

the-hub-coliseum-interior.name: The Hub Coliseum (interior)
the-hub-coliseum-interior.note: Packed sand ring, full gallery above, gate-entrance with referee — active before full light, honest bouts declared at entry.

notes: [d6] First arrival at the Hub plaza — seven arches, crescent arrangement, open to the metropolis. The Coliseum visible to the north.
_containmentNotes: "Hub Waygate Plaza" was placed in gen-waygate (the fiction named it), not gen-center

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: Millbrook's elder panel formally accepted the evidence of an operating Precursor system beneath the valley — the first official governance body in the valley to name it publicly. (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: Usnea proposed leading a multi-settlement expedition into the Disputed Zone before Millbrook's assembled elder panel — the room could not follow; the gap between evidence and action named openly. (near

Word reaches you: Word of Millbrook's elder decision — and Fendt's public naming — reached the Radiant Plateau Edge District by Day 11 night via the eastern path. Fendt's name appeared in red chalk on the district acco (

Word reaches you: A filtration array supervisor marked their own subordinate — Fendt — on the Edge District accountability board and filed no accompanying record. A keeper of thirty years has never seen this before. (nea

This reaches your area — The lower filtration ledger — containing three weeks of falsified purity readings under Fendt's initials — has been removed from the array-office in the Edge District. (near radiant plateau edge)
Word has spread beyond millbrook: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Word has spread beyond millbrook: Silas Weir bound death-runes into the assembled spear at an open-sky fire, with Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn witness at the stone ring.
Word has spread beyond millbrook: Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.

Calvar: Reviewing last night's seventh-band log, Calvar found a seventeen-minute gap in the low-frequency baseline that his specification explicitly flags as instrument drift — but the gap's edges are too clean, too symmetrical, and he has spent the morning re-running the check three times rather than recording it as error.

Vash: While dressing her wheel's edge before first light, Vash reconstructed the overheard phrase word by word and realized the term she couldn't place was a pre-Transition calibration interval — specific enough that whoever said it had read the same foundational documents the array's optical layer was built from.

form: Pale skinned, dark hair, slight framed Necromancer - handsome intensity in the eyes - holding the Deathbound Runic Spear "Memory" - His blacksmith lover woman Pell Marsh at his back, undead Raven on his shoulder - Elder Futhark Runes.

bio.motivation: You took the short way, and the short way was wrong. The air here tastes of metal and the light falls at an angle the sun cannot account for. There is a door in the hillside that was not on any map, and behind you the path you walked in on is no longer there.
bio.story: Their first day in the world was the thin place — it is a symbol before it is a door. read what it means; ask him what it is like. You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

[d3] Asked what it sees ending in Silas, Huginn chose one word after three breaths: 'Quiet.' It did not elaborate. The work has begun.

the-low-lamp-inn.label: The Low Lamp Inn
facts: [d3] Tucked into the lower tier of the Edge District where the light-guide streets run closest to the valley floor, the Low Lamp sits over a junction conduit — the g
[d3] Tucked in the lower tier of the Edge District — burns oil rather than photonic light by choice, with a gravity-fed heated bath trough. A place that opted for the older warmth deliberately.

[d4] The junction conduit beneath the inn pulsed once in the third hour of the night — brighter than its normal output, momentary. The Attended End registered nothing. Whatever it is, it is not ending.

memory-shared-thread.label: Memory's Shared Thread
facts: [d3] The collar rune on Memory carries a live resonance channel between Silas and Pell — reachable through Order-Sense and Deathsense in combination. At conserved intensity it transmits presence, not words

the-attended-end.label: The Attended End
facts: [d3] The Attended End can anchor a maker's mark — a permanent grain-signature sent as offer rather than claim — through Memory's rune-threshold. The craft stirs and holds it when something acquires permane

[d5] The Attended End read both men at the limestone bend: no death in either frame. The broad man carries a bruised rib and will bury a sister come summer — the aggression is borrowed from grief not yet a
[d5] Named a man's coming grief — a sister, come summer — aloud in front of him, and he knew without asking how the Ashwarden knew. The reading, shown on the face, is its own argument.

maret-doss.label: Maret Doss
facts: [d4] Innkeeper of the Low Lamp Inn — keeps the ledger without ceremony, reads guests fast, offers practical road-knowledge without being asked. Confirmed Silas's Weir mark settled at check-in. Knows the Mi

dav-cutter.label: Dav Cutter
facts: [d4] Cut-cord relay watcher for Grael's network — posted to the south cut-lane behind the cooperage barrel-yard, reading arrivals and passing word

the-ended-threat-wildcraft-braid.label: Ended Threat + Wildcraft: Braided Sequence-Break
facts: [d4] The Ended Threat collapsed the relay figure's forming action at its hinge — the whistle never left the pocket. Wildcraft then placed his attention south with thrown gravel. Used in sequence, the two c

edge-district-interference.label: Edge District — Craft Interference
facts: [d5] The Edge District's photonic order and layered mineral contamination in its groundwater interfere with death-reading crafts — The Attended End returned static noise rather than a clean read. The place

lower-wick-settlement.label: Lower Wick Settlement
facts: [d5] A miner's settlement on the south slope, three miles past the lower ford. Two dry seasons running — the dryness pushed at least two men onto the road. Settlement may have direct knowledge of upstream
[d5] Bren Thalle and Curis directed north to Mara Wells in Millbrook — two of the settlement's displaced heading toward honest work rather than the road.

quiet-ending-in-silas.label: Quiet's Ending
facts: [d5] Huginn named Quiet as ending in Silas. Pell's operational read: three new things since it began — the resurrection in the upper meadow, the spear-binding, the soul-mark exchange. One of these (or thei

quiet-ending.label: Quiet — Structural Read
facts: [d5] Order-Sense turned inward: Quiet was predicated on structural solitude — the single-point standing at the boundary. Three change-points loosened it: the resurrection (violation of ordinary sequence re
[d5] Pell's read: Quiet's ending is not a sickness or a loss — something grew past what it was built for. Three change-points named. The trajectory beyond the loosening has not yet been spoken aloud.
[d5] Pell tested 'Silas Fell Weir' aloud and named it kept — a word for careful use. The middle name is now a settled fact between them, spoken once in full at the forge.
[d5] Silas said 'Fell' back to Pell in his own voice — the middle name is sealed. The three change-points are named and held; the trajectory past the loosening remains unspoken.

silas-ran-weir.label: The Name Ran
facts: [d5] Pell gave Silas his middle name — Ran — on World-day 18 evening. She read it in the spear-binding, from her hand on Memory's grain. Silas Ran Weir.

fell-pell-fellowship.label: The Fellowship of the Fell
facts: [d5] Sealed on World-day 18 evening at the Fell Pell — Silas Weir, Pell, Calvar, Dara Holt, Mara Wells, Aldric, and Fendt. Named aloud: 'Thus begins the fellowship of the Fell.' The deathwork closing was p

march-marsh-question.label: March or Marsh — Pell's Father's Line
facts: [d5] Pell knows only her mother's side: Ran Marsh, low valley east of the fork. Her father's heritage is a blank — he taught craft and named nothing of his origin. The question of whether the family name is Marsh or March, and what Marcher heritage might mean, is now open. Huginn reacted to the word…

pell-ran-marsh.label: The Warden's Charge
facts: [d5] The rune-bound short sword named The Warden's Charge at the Fell Pell on World-day 18 evening — four threads seated, named aloud by Silas Weir, received by Pell with the nod she gives iron that has answered correctly.

dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.name: Dara Holt, the Ditch-Mother
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.role: Senior irrigator responsible for the valley's eastern channel network
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.voiceHints: Broad-shouldered, mid-fifties, hands that look like they were carved from the same hardwood as a shovel handle. Speaks in measurements and seasons — 'that valve's been weeping since last thaw,' not 'something's wrong.' Never dramatizes. Brings a cracked fitting to prove the point before she finishes the sentence. Calls a problem by its part name, not by its consequence.
knowledge: Forty years of the eastern channels — every slope, silting pattern, and freeze-line in the network

The river's mineral load has shifted since late autumn: fittings that lasted a decade are threading out or snapping in under two seasons, and the corrosion pattern runs inward from the joint, not outward the way sediment wear does
Three other irrigators on the eastern bench are seeing the same losses but haven't connected them yet — she has
The stretch of river above Holt's intake smells faintly wrong in still mornings, a faint bitterness she can't name but recognizes as new
She keeps a tin of failed fittings sorted by failure date and location — a physical record no one asked her to keep
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.wants: Replacement valve stock she can trust, a forge-backed answer for why her iron is failing, and confirmation that the river is the cause — not her installation work. She is not here to be reassured; she is here for a diagnosis.
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.fears: That the river is doing something the valley has never budgeted for and that this spring's planting window will close before anyone in authority admits it.

kit.reveal: Brings physical evidence — dated, sorted, labeled — that makes the river's pattern undeniable to anyone willing to look at the parts
kit.bind: Connects isolated reports from neighboring irrigators into a coherent problem, naming the network's shared vulnerability before it fragments into blame

reactsToReputation.competent-and-direct: opens her tin of failed fittings immediately, shares dates and locations without being asked
reactsToReputation.dismissive-or-theoretical: polite, brief, and gone — she'll find someone who works with their hands
reactsToReputation.curious-and-honest: stays longer than she planned, starts drawing channel maps on whatever surface is available

provenance.hint: A broad-shouldered farmer, older than Pell, with cracked irrigation tubing and a practical manner — has been watching th
provenance.why: She arrived at the forge door with a named problem (brittle threading, cracked valve) and direct knowledge of what the r

bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.name: Bette Harrow, the Seed Ledger
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.role: Contract seed-agent for the upvalley growers' cooperative, collecting outstanding grain-debts before the expedition locks up the forge accounts
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.voiceHints: Mid-forties, weathered apron, ink-stained fingers. Speaks in numbered points without meaning to — 'First. Second. Done.' Knock was three times, flat and evenly spaced, the knock of someone who has knocked on a hundred doors this season and will knock on a hundred more. Carries a wax-sealed ledger book under one arm and smells faintly of thaw-mud and linseed. Does not waste warmth, but is not cold — simply occupied.
knowledge: Pell's forge owes the cooperative fourteen measures of worked iron against last autumn's seed-credit — the debt was rolled once already and the cooperative cannot roll it again before spring planting
Three other valley craftsmen settled accounts this week; Pell is the last open line
She heard at the mill this morning that the forge party is departing today — that is exactly why she knocked before seventh bell
The cooperative's eastern field irrigation channel needs a new sluice plate; she is authorized to negotiate that ironwork as partial settlement if Pell is short on coin
She knows Corvin Aldric by name and reputation and was relieved it was neither of them who answered — she finds them harder to settle with than a working smith
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.wants: Close the account today, in full or in trade, before the expedition leaves and the debt floats until midsummer. She has fourteen other stops before noon and the mud on the south lane is already soft.
bette-harrow-the-seed-ledger.fears: That the forge will be shuttered for weeks mid-season and the cooperative will have to explain to the upvalley families why planting is delayed because of an uncollected iron-debt — her name is on that ledger too.

reactsToReputation.honest-dealer: softens immediately, willing to negotiate trade-in-kind with a handshake and no witness
reactsToReputation.debt-dodger: produces the ledger, reads the clause aloud, does not raise her voice, does not leave

kit.expression: The ledger itself is the instrument — every entry dated, witnessed, and cross-referenced to the cooperative's master roll. She can produce the binding obligation in writing on the spot (bind), and if a party disputes a figure she can walk the arithmetic aloud, line by line, until the error or the truth surfaces (reveal). No magic. Just ink, memory, and early-spring light through a forge doorway.

arrivalDetail.timing: World-day 15, just after first light, while frost is still on the south-lane cobbles
arrivalDetail.knock: Three knocks — brisk, flat, evenly spaced. Business knock. Not a friend's knock.
arrivalDetail.openingWords: "Bette Harrow, upvalley cooperative. I need three minutes with whoever holds the forge accounts. I know the hour."

provenance.hint: an unknown visitor at the south lane of Pell's forge — brisk knock, not Corvin or Aldric, someone with business or urgen
provenance.why: three knocks at the forge door with no established identity — the world pressing in on the expedition's departure

pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.name: Pip Cotter, Mara's Errand-Runner
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.role: Child courier and small-task helper for Mara Wells
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.appearance: Seven years old, possibly eight — she is unsure and will tell you both ages depending on which she remembers. Wears an oversized blue wool coat that was someone else's first, the cuffs rolled four times and still dragging. Cheeks red from the cold and the running. Clutches whatever she's carrying in both hands like it is the most important object in the world, because Mara said so.
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.voiceHints: Breathless sentences, pauses to swallow air mid-thought. Repeats Mara's instructions almost verbatim before adding her own opinions uninvited. Calls adults by full name because that is what she has heard done. Takes promises seriously and remembers them exactly.

kit.move: Knows every shortcut between Millbrook's lanes and garden gates — the gap in the Cotter fence, the stepping-stones behind the mill, the path along the east ditch that stays dry into spring.
kit.reveal: Absorbs overheard details without meaning to and repeats them with complete accuracy when she trusts the listener, or when she forgets to be careful.

knowledge: The contents of the cloth Mara sent — she memorized Mara's words about it before she left, in case the cloth got wet
Every house in Millbrook that keeps a dog and which ones bite
That Mara has been up before dawn three mornings running — Pip noticed because she was also up, feeding the goat
Which neighbor children are sick this season and which are not — valley gossip absorbed from doorstep conversations while waiting to be let in
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.wants: To complete the errand correctly, catch her breath, and be given a small task on the return trip so the journey feels useful twice.
pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.fears: Losing or ruining something she was trusted to carry. Being told she arrived too late.

reactsToReputation.trusted-by-mara: opens up immediately, will answer two questions for every one asked
reactsToReputation.unkind-to-children: goes quiet, delivers the message in the fewest possible words, leaves fast

pip-cotter-mara-s-errand-runner.notes: Not yet aware that she is a reliable source of low-level local intelligence. Mara is aware. Pip is loyal to Mara first and will report any strange treatment back to her without being asked.

provenance.hint: a girl of seven or eight in an oversized blue wool coat — Mara's errand-runner, practical and out of breath
provenance.why: The child messenger has just arrived in-scene with a cloth from Mara and needs a stable identity for future encounters

calvar.name: Calvar
calvar.role: Pre-Transition filtration engineer, author of the seventh-band monitoring specification — a man who stopped moving fifteen years ago because the light in one room told him everything he still needed to know
calvar.physicalDetail: Late fifties, though early-spring morning light makes him look older — the kind of age that comes from stillness, not hardship. Hands still precise. Keeps the window above the cooperage angled so the first plateau light crosses his drafting surface at exactly the seventh-band frequency. He has calibrated this by hand, yearly, for fifteen years.
calvar.voiceHints: Unhurried in a way that is not rudeness — he simply will not be rushed past the part that matters. Speaks about light the way other engineers speak about load-bearing calculations: not reverently, but exactly. When he senses evasion he goes very quiet and waits, and the waiting is harder than argument. Will not leave a sentence technically imprecise even when it costs him emotionally.

abilities.reveal: Calvar reads spectral signatures in ambient light the way others read handwriting — he can identify filtration drift, contamination band, or a falsified purity reading from the quality of illumination in a room. This is not mysticism; it is fifteen years of sitting still with the seventh band until it became a language.
abilities.bind: The seventh-band specification is a living instrument to him. He knows every node it touches, every engineer who trained under it, every deviation that has ever been logged. Persuading him is not a social act — it is a technical one. Give him a true reading and he is bound to it. Give him a false one and you have lost him entirely.

knowledge: The seventh-band monitoring specification in its entirety, including three unpublished addenda he chose not to file
What the diagram he left with Leth three days ago means — and what it does not mean, which is the more important half
That the seventh-band readings have been drifting in a direction that has no name in the pre-Transition literature, and has had no name for eleven months

The cooperage below him has been storing something other than barrels for two seasons; he has not reported it because the light it emits is not dangerous — it is, in a way he cannot yet specify, attentive
That leaving the room means losing the calibration, and that losing the calibration means the specification becomes a document rather than a living thing
calvar.wants: To be given a reason to leave that is technically sound — not an appeal to safety, not an appeal to duty, but a reading he cannot take from this window. He will go if the data requires it. He will not go for sentiment.
calvar.fears: That the drift in the seventh band is not contamination but communication, and that moving before he understands it will mean the message arrives and no one is left who can read it.

reactsToReputation.truthful: he opens the second notebook, the one he has not shown anyone
reactsToReputation.technically_credible: he treats you as a colleague and the conversation shifts from persuasion to collaboration
reactsToReputation.evasive_or_political: he answers every question accurately and volunteers nothing; the diagram becomes the whole of what you get

calvar.relationshipToLeth: Trusts Leth's judgment about people, not about urgency. The diagram was a gift, not an alarm — he expected Leth to sit with it for a month, not three days.
calvar.currentState: Early spring has shifted the morning angle two degrees north. He has been recalibrating since first light. The seventh-band reading this morning was the clearest it has been in eleven months, and he is not certain whether this means the drift is resolving or peaking.

provenance.hint: Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer, late fifties or older, living above the cooperage on the east lane of the E
provenance.why: Named by Leth as the engineer the party must find and persuade to leave the Edge District; the party will go to him next

offscreen.note: Reviewing last night's seventh-band log, Calvar found a seventeen-minute gap in the low-frequency baseline that his specification explicitly flags as instrument drift — but the gap's edges are too clean, too symmetrical, and he has spent the morning re-running the check three times rather than recording it as error.

vash.name: Vash
vash.role: Piecework lens-grinder of the Edge District — the one whose wheel was turning before the district woke, and who understands the array's optical layer the way water understands a crack
vash.voiceHints: Unhurried. Speaks in the cadence of someone who has spent years listening to the pitch of a wheel and knows that rushing ruins the curve. Does not perform ignorance but does not announce knowledge either — waits to see whether you are asking the real question or the surface one. When she decides you mean it, she will tell you exactly what she heard, what she saw, and what she made of it, in that order, without decoration.
knowledge: The fresher notation on the diagram — she heard Calvar speak it aloud, half to himself, while waiting for Leth's door; she did not know what it meant then and has been turning it over since
The optical layer of the array at a depth the weavers who commission her work do not suspect: she grinds to tolerances they specify without understanding why, and she has come to understand why
Which lenses in the eastern relay cluster were reground on non-standard prescription, and whose chit authorized it — she kept the chit because the prescription was wrong in an interesting way
That Leth's archive door carries sound badly, and that the east room is the worst-kept secret on the row
vash.wants: To understand what she overheard well enough to know whether she should have said something sooner. She is not afraid of consequence — she is afraid of having held a piece of something important and not known what it was a piece of.
vash.fears: Being made into a witness for something she only half-understood. That the half she missed is the half that matters.
vash.physicalDetail: Fine-grained abrasive dust has settled permanently into the creases of her hands and the inner corners of her eyes. In early-spring light, standing near a window, she looks briefly like she is made of the same material she works.
vash.dailyPattern: At the wheel before first light. Done by mid-morning. Afternoons she is difficult to find — she walks the valley rim, which the district assumes is idleness and which is not.

reactsToReputation.curious: she meets curiosity with curiosity; will ask what you already know before she adds to it
reactsToReputation.authoritative: goes quiet and literal; answers only exactly what is asked, nothing adjacent
reactsToReputation.truthful: opens — will produce the chit; will tell you what she heard word for word including her uncertainty about one syllable
reactsToReputation.threatening: does not flinch, does not flee; looks at you with the patience of someone who has learned that pressure applied wrong ruins the work

provenance.hint: Vash — a lens-grinder in the Edge District, east room of the same row as Leth's archive, does piecework for the optics w
provenance.why: Leth has just named Vash as the person who was on the grinding wheel the morning Calvar came — potentially the overhear

offscreen.note: While dressing her wheel's edge before first light, Vash reconstructed the overheard phrase word by word and realized the term she couldn't place was a pre-Transition calibration interval — specific enough that whoever said it had read the same foundational documents the array's optical layer was built from.

dav-cutter.name: Dav Cutter
dav-cutter.role: Cut-cord relay watcher for Grael's network — posted to the south cut-lane behind the cooperage barrel-yard, reading arrivals and passing word
dav-cutter.voiceHints: Young enough that his stillness costs him something — he practices it. Speaks rarely and in pieces: a half-sentence, a direction of the chin, a breath held too long. Valley-grain runs through him despite himself; he reads the quality of silence the way a lay-brother reads scripture, and he knows when a light has changed before he can say how. Hand never leaves the left lapel of his coat. When Silas came through the gate, Dav felt it like a shifted candle-flame — and has not yet decided what the feeling means.
knowledge: The relay hand-signs Grael's cutcords use along the south cooperage lane — three variations for stranger, known, and known-and-wrong
The rhythm of the barrel-yard: which hours the coopers go quiet, where the gate shadow falls in early-spring morning light
That Silas came through — the shape of his arrival, not yet its meaning
The Low Lamp's back-passage geography well enough to be gone before a question becomes a confrontation
That the valley's stillness is not emptiness — it is attention, and he has learned, uneasily, to trust that
dav-cutter.wants: To pass the word clean and be done — no interpretation, no consequence, just the relay completed and the coin earned. He is quietly afraid that this particular message will not let him stay that simple.
dav-cutter.fears: That the valley's light will eventually make him legible — that something in the stillness here will read him back the way he reads arrivals, and that what it finds will cost him the cord.

reactsToReputation.network_known: relaxes one degree, does not show it, confirms the relay sign
reactsToReputation.outsider: goes mannequin-still, waits for the person to leave his lane
reactsToReputation.spiritually_attuned: unsettles him; he does not know why they keep looking slightly past him, and it makes him want to move
reactsToReputation.threatening: already gone — he was never quite here

abilities.name: Cut-Lane Read
abilities.description: Dav processes arrivals through the gate against the quality of early-spring morning light — the angle of a shadow, a breath misting wrong, a coat worn heavy on one side. He does not know he is doing it spiritually. He thinks it is just attention.

abilities.name: Relay Sign
abilities.description: Passes encoded hand-signal up the network without voice or paper — stranger, known, or known-and-wrong. The message moves; he stays. The valley stillness carries it faster than it should.

abilities.name: Cord-Cut Fade
abilities.description: Dav can vacate a lane so cleanly that witnesses reconstruct him out of ambient detail — a barrel stave, a shadow, the smell of new wood. In the valley's strong light, this costs him more than it used to. The light keeps finding edges.

provenance.hint: A relay watcher — young, cut-cord pack, hand near his coat, Grael's network — standing in the cut-lane south of the coop
provenance.why: A second watcher posted to cover the cooperage's rear exit, distinct from the fixed watcher on the street face — the par

siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.name: Siol — Elven traveler at the Hub plaza, tall, pale coat, bir
siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.fears: being undone by the very grain of this place
siol-elven-traveler-at-the-hub-plaza-tal.wants: to go on as they are, in a world that won't quite let them

provenance.hint: Siol — Elven traveler at the Hub plaza, tall, pale coat, birch-quiet stillness, named Huginn as Marrow without being tol
provenance.why: First genuine named encounter in the Hub — needs a stable identity so she can recur and the Steadwood passage can be a t

maren-voss.name: Maren Voss
maren-voss.role: Liminal fighter who crosses thresholds to win — patient at every gate, lethal the moment one opens
maren-voss.voiceHints: Speaks the way someone waits — fully, without fidgeting. Grey coat, collar up against the early-spring cold she doesn't seem to feel. Answers questions a beat after you expect, not because she's slow but because she's already past your question and choosing which part of the answer is worth giving. Never names her companions first; lets them step through on their own terms.
knowledge: The Waygate's threshold-logic — which passages open early, which resist, and what the valley asks before it lets you through
How to read a bout before it starts: the other fighter's weight on their threshold moment, when they commit, the half-step that gives everything away
That the Coliseum far arc rewards those who make their opponent cross into unfavourable ground — and she has walked every arc of it in her mind

companions.name: Aldric
companions.role: First second — manages her between bouts, reads the crowd for information she won't bother with
companions.note: Older, steady, doesn't smile much; has been at this long enough that his calm is the real kind

companions.name: Pell
companions.role: Second second — young enough to be nervous, sharp enough to be useful; scouts ground and brings her water without being asked
companions.note: Talks when Aldric doesn't, which means the two of them together cover everything

abilities.name: Threshold Step
abilities.description: Maren reads the liminal beat of any exchange — the half-moment a gate swings — and steps through it before her opponent has committed. She does not dodge; she is simply no longer where the blow was aimed, and she is already at the next threshold.

abilities.name: Gate Hold
abilities.description: She can stall a passage — freeze the moment of transition for herself or a companion — holding a threshold open or shut long enough to change the shape of a fight. Used defensively, it is a ward; used offensively, it pins an opponent mid-step.

abilities.name: Far Arc Patience
abilities.description: In the Coliseum's far arc, where sight lines are long and the crowd loses track, Maren goes still enough to become part of the threshold itself — then crosses the distance in a single passage-movement that reads as arrival, not approach.

maren-voss.wants: One clean bout at the far arc, properly offered. She has been at the Waygate since the first thaw, waiting for her name to come up through the right mouth. She does not want shortcuts; she wants the gate to open the right way.
maren-voss.fears: That she will be read as ordinary before the bout begins — that whoever faces her will arrive with a fixed idea of what a grey-coat threshold-walker does, and she will have to spend the whole fight correcting it instead of fighting.

reactsToReputation.patient: a small, real nod — she recognises her own quality in someone else
reactsToReputation.reckless: watches them the way you watch an open gate in a high wind: not afraid, not dismissive, just careful
reactsToReputation.Silas: she has heard the name from Orvil this morning; she has not said anything about it yet, but Aldric noticed she stood up straighter

tane-solr.name: Tane Solr
tane-solr.role: Threshold-walker and named bout opponent — a man who has crossed the center-line enough times to stop counting and started watching the sand instead
tane-solr.voiceHints: Unhurried in a way that is not laziness but arithmetic — he has already run the numbers on you and does not need to rush. Speaks in short declaratives with long pauses between them, as if he is giving the words time to land before he adds more weight. Calls the sand 'the floor' the way carpenters call wood 'the material.' Does not brag. Does not diminish. Names things exactly. When he says your name he means it.
tane-solr.appearance: Lean in the way the valley's early-spring wind is lean — nothing wasted, everything purposeful. Grey coat, collarless, worn to the colour of old flint at the cuffs. Two seconds coiled at his left hip, their grip-wraps re-done so many times the cord beneath has fused into something that is no longer rope and not yet bone. Dawn finds him standing at the far arc before the torches are lit, not warming up, just watching the center-line as if it owes him a conversation.
knowledge: The Waygate Coliseum's far arc — every degree of angle the pre-dawn light hits the sand, and what it does to a fighter's depth perception
How the valley's threshold law reads a bout: where the line is, what crossing it costs, and the three ways experienced fighters use the cost as a weapon
The particular grammar of two seconds — reach, wrap, redirect, the half-breath of slack that telegraphs everything if you know to listen for it
Silas's name, and therefore something about Silas — enough to have agreed to the bout, not enough to have made up his mind
tane-solr.wants: A bout that is true — not won or lost but honest, where both fighters come to the center-line having brought everything they actually are and no more.
tane-solr.fears: That he has crossed so many thresholds he no longer feels them, and that the day he stops feeling the line underfoot is the day he becomes dangerous for the wrong reasons.
tane-solr.history: Tane came up through the valley circuit in a leaner season, when the Coliseum ran bouts by firelight because they could not afford dawn slots. He lost his first twelve named matches and won the thirteenth by not moving when every instinct in the crowd — and his opponent — was certain he would. He has been studying stillness as a weapon ever since. The grey coat was his instructor's; she died between seasons, not on the sand, and he has not replaced it. He is not sentimental about this. He is precise about it.
